paper_title: Bridging Data Gaps in Diffusion Models with Adversarial Noise-Based Transfer Learning
paper_abstract: Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) show significant potential in image generation, yet their performance hinges on having access to large datasets. Previous works, like Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), have tackled the limited data problem by transferring pretrained models learned with sufficient data. However, those methods are hard to utilize in DPMs because of the distinct differences between DPM-based and GANbased methods, which show the integral of the unique iterative denoising process and the need for many time steps with no target noise in DPMs. In this paper, we propose a novel DPM-based transfer learning method, called DPMs-ANT, to address the limited data problem. It includes two strategies: similarity-guided training, which boosts transfer with a classifier, and adversarial noise selection, which adaptively chooses targeted noise based on the input image. Extensive experiments in the context of few-shot image generation tasks demonstrate that our method is efficient and excels in terms of image quality and diversity compared to existing GAN-based and DPM-based methods.

techniques.name: DPMs-ANT
techniques.description:
DPMs-ANT (Diffusion Probabilistic Models with Adversarial Noise-based Transfer learning) is an end-to-end few-shot transfer learning framework that fine-tunes a pretrained diffusion model on a small target dataset by integrating similarity-guided training, adversarial noise selection, and lightweight adapter layers on the U-Net backbone. In similarity-guided training, a pretrained classifier pφ is leveraged to measure the domain gap at each diffusion timestep t by computing the gradients ∇_{x_t} log pφ(y=S|x_t) and ∇_{x_t} log pφ(y=T|x_t) for the source and target domains, respectively, and formulating a training loss as the KL divergence between the conditional reverse distributions p_{θ_S,φ}(x_{t-1}|x_t,y=S) and p_{θ_T,φ}(x_{t-1}|x_t,y=T). In adversarial noise selection, the fine-tuning objective is cast as a min–max problem:  
minimize_θ maximize_{ε} E_{t,x0}[||ε – ε_θ(x_t,t) – σ_t^2γ ∇_{x_t} log pφ(y=T|x_t)||^2],  
where ε_θ denotes the model’s noise prediction, σ_t^2γ ∇_{x_t} log pφ(y=T|x_t) is the similarity-corrected gradient term, and the inner maximization over ε is approximated via J steps of gradient ascent  
ε_{j+1} = Norm(ε_j + ω ∇_{ε_j}[–||ε_j – ε_θ(x_t,t) – σ_t^2γ ∇_{x_t} log pφ(y=T|x_t)||^2]),  
with ω as the “learning rate” of the negative loss, Norm(·) enforcing zero mean and unit variance, ε_0 ∼ N(0,I), and t ∼ Uniform({1,…,T}). Lightweight adapter layers inserted into the U-Net restrict updates to a small parameter subset, reducing memory and compute overhead. This procedure requires only a few hundred iterations to converge, preserves image diversity, and yields high-quality samples in the target style.

components.name: Similarity-Guided Training
components.description:
Similarity-Guided Training introduces a modified diffusion model loss that incorporates a domain similarity term derived from a fixed, pretrained binary classifier pₚₕᵢ(y|xₜ) to align a source-trained model to a target domain. At each diffusion timestep t, given a noised sample xₜ obtained by adding ε∼N(0,I) to an original sample x₀ and a pretrained noise predictor ε_θ(xₜ,t), the training objective is defined as  
Lᵗ(θ) = E_{x₀,t,ε}‖ε – ε_θ(xₜ,t) – σₜ²·γ·∇_{xₜ} log pₚₕᵢ(y=T|xₜ)‖²,  
where σₜ² denotes the diffusion noise variance and γ is a hyperparameter controlling the strength of similarity guidance. The classifier gradient ∇_{xₜ} log pₚₕᵢ(y=T|xₜ) serves as an indirect measure of distance to the target domain, gently steering the model toward target-like reconstructions without overfitting to scarce clean target images. Minimizing this augmented loss effectively bridges the domain gap and bolsters few-shot transfer learning in diffusion models.

components.name: Adversarial Noise Selection
components.description:
Adversarial Noise Selection is a min–max optimization strategy designed to identify and minimize the “worst-case” Gaussian noise under which a pretrained diffusion model fails to denoise a target sample. Formally, the objective is  
minimize over θ, maximize over ε∼N(0,I) of E_{x₀,t} ‖ε – ε_θ(√āₜ x₀ + √(1–āₜ) ε, t)‖²,  
where the inner maximization over ε is intractable in closed form and is therefore solved approximately by J steps of gradient ascent:  
ε_{j+1} = Norm(ε_j + ω·∇_{ε_j}‖ε_j – ε_θ(√āₜ x₀ + √(1–āₜ) ε_j, t)‖²),  
with ε₀ sampled from N(0,I), ω denoting the noise ascent step size (i.e., the “learning rate” of the negative loss), and Norm(·) re-normalizing ε to zero mean and unit variance. After a model has been trained on a large source dataset—thereby acquiring strong generic denoising capability—this procedure concentrates exclusively on those Gaussian noise realizations that the model still struggles to remove on the target domain samples. By focusing training on the most adversarial noise instances, Adversarial Noise Selection not only reduces unbalanced gradient noise across samples and accelerates convergence but, by minimizing the worst-case noise (which subsumes all “better” noise cases), it also more accurately corrects gradients computed with limited data and effectively mitigates underfitting in few-shot transfer learning.

components.name: Adapter Module for U-Net Fine-Tuning
components.description: The Adapter Module for U-Net Fine-Tuning introduces a lightweight residual adapter into each layer of a pretrained U-Net, such that at the l-th layer the representation is given by xₜˡ = θˡ(xₜˡ⁻¹) + ψˡ(xₜˡ⁻¹), where θˡ denotes the frozen pretrained weights and ψˡ is a compact adapter composed of a down-projection, nonlinearity and up-projection in the style of Houlsby et al. (2019). During the entire fine-tuning process, all θ parameters remain fixed and only the adapter parameters ψ are updated. These adapter parameters are trained under the similarity-guided diffusion loss (Equation 5) together with the adversarial-noise selection objective, enabling efficient domain transfer from source to target with minimal additional computation and memory overhead—requiring on the order of only 300 iterations and approximately 3 GPU hours—while preserving the bulk of the pretrained model’s capacity.

findings: Non-Targeted Noise Imbalance: The fully random Gaussian noise in diffusion models imposes unbalanced effects on different samples during few-shot adaptation: some samples underfit (remain in source domain) while others overfit (become indistinguishable from the few target images). This divergent transfer pace leads to noisy gradients and requires many iterations to correct when only limited target data are available.

Effectiveness of Similarity Guidance on Noised Images: Indirectly guiding diffusion model adaptation via a classifier gradient on noised images (rather than comparing final clean images) stabilizes the learning direction, prevents overfitting to few-shot target images, and improves diversity compared to naive fine-tuning of the diffusion loss.

Adversarial Noise Accelerates Convergence: Minimizing the model’s response to the worst-case Gaussian noise drastically reduces the number of fine-tuning iterations from thousands to a few hundred, while preserving or improving target-style fidelity and sample diversity.

Lightweight Adapters Save Compute and Memory: Inserting small residual adapter modules into the pretrained U-Net and freezing its original weights achieves comparable or better few-shot adaptation quality (FID and LPIPS) with only ~6% of the trainable parameters and ~50% of the GPU memory and compute compared to full-fine-tuning.

Classifier Sufficiency with 10 Images: A binary domain classifier trained on only 10 noised target shots yields robust gradients for similarity-guided adaptation, matching or exceeding the performance obtained with classifiers trained on 100 images.
